learning.summary: Application OS compatibility means an application must be built to run on the operating system that will execute it. A program file that works on one OS usually cannot be moved to a different OS and run directly, even if both systems can store or open the same data files.
learning.explanation: An operating system is more than a place where files are stored. It provides the services, file formats, libraries, permissions, system calls, and runtime environment that applications depend on when they launch and run.

facts.text: Applications must be written or packaged for the operating system they are expected to run on.
Executable files are generally OS-specific and cannot usually be moved from one operating system to another and run directly.
Application compatibility and file compatibility are different: an OS may be able to store or transfer a file even when it cannot run the application that created it.
A compatible application can interact correctly with the operating system platform it targets.
Compatibility layers, virtualization, containers, and web apps can change how software runs across platforms, but they are exceptions or extensions to the core OS compatibility rule.

examples.text: A Windows executable should not be expected to run directly on Linux just because the file can be copied to the Linux system.
examples.context: Shows the difference between being able to store or transfer a file and being able to execute it.

examples.text: A document, image, or text file may be usable across multiple operating systems if compatible applications exist on each system.
examples.context: Shows that data/file portability is separate from application executable portability.

notes: Later knowledge objects should link this concept to compatibility layers, virtualization, containers, and web applications.

examTips.text: Do not assume that copying an application executable to another operating system makes it runnable there.
Separate questions about running an application from questions about opening, saving, or transferring data files.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking that all files are the same as executable programs.
Assuming an application that runs on Windows will also run directly on Linux or macOS.
Confusing file/data compatibility with application/OS compatibility.

scenarios.situation: A user copies a Windows program file to a Linux computer and expects it to launch.
scenarios.expectedAction: Identify the correct concept or response.

scenarios.situation: A user can open the same document on multiple operating systems but cannot run the same installer on all of them.
scenarios.expectedAction: Identify the correct concept or response.

pbqIdeas.task: Given several files and operating systems, sort which items are likely applications that require OS-specific compatibility and which are portable data files.

questionTargets: Identify why an executable from one OS may not run on another OS.
Distinguish application compatibility from file or data compatibility.
Recognize that applications depend on OS platform services.

related.reason: Application compatibility depends on the operating system platform.
Executable files are OS-specific application artifacts.

contrastsWith.reason: File or data compatibility is separate from whether an application executable can run on an OS.

reviewNotes: Source-supported: applications must be specifically written to run in the target OS.
Source-supported: a Windows executable should not be expected to run directly in Linux.
Source-supported requirement preserved: distinguish application compatibility from file/data compatibility.

Enriched content not directly supported by the provided evidence: references to libraries, permissions, system calls, runtime environment, installers, and cross-platform data files are included as general explanatory framing and should be reviewed.
Gap handling: placeholders for compatibility layers, virtualization, containers, and web apps are included only as future related concepts, not as full exception coverage.
